Article.ArticleTitle: Virtual simulation accurately predicts in vivo flow diverter length for intracranial aneurysm treatment: a multicenter validation study.

Abstract.AbstractText: Accurate device sizing is crucial for successful flow diverter (FD) therapy in intracranial aneurysms. This study assesses the accuracy and clinical utility of the Ankyras virtual simulation software (Mentice AB, Gothenburg, Sweden) in predicting FD length across multiple device types and manufacturers.

We retrospectively analyzed 193 FDs (7 device types) deployed in 180 patients with 230 intracranial aneurysms. Simulation-based prediction of effective lengths (simulated length (SL)) and nominal manufacturer specifications (labeled length) were compared with measured in vivo lengths (measured length (ML)). Performance was evaluated using simulation accuracy (SA), absolute error (AE), relative error (RE), length ratio, and correlation analysis.

Virtual simulation demonstrated good clinical usability, achieving a mean absolute deviation of only -1.38 mm at the proximal landing zone compared with ML. Simulation-based predictions showed significantly superior accuracy compared with nominal manufacturer specifications (SA 89.6±11.3% vs 81.8±13.7%, P<0.001), with mean AE reduced by 58% (-1.38 mm vs -3.34 mm) and mean RE by 42% (10.5% vs 18.2%). Strong correlations between SL and ML (r=0.900) validated predictive reliability across all tested device types. Centerline correction technology further enhanced parameters such as SA to 94.8±9.5%.

Virtual simulation-based prediction of effective FD length using the Ankyras software showed reliable and clinically meaningful results, enabling accurate estimations of the proximal landing zone and overall length. Ankyras, along with comparable simulation platforms, may provide considerable potential to facilitate FD selection and implementation, especially in anatomically complex cases.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Competing interests: MRo, TL, MRe, BM, CN, FS, and SW declare no competing interests. CM reports grants or contracts from Phenox/Wallaby and eppdata GmbH; consulting fees from Phenox/Wallaby and eppdata GmbH; honoraria for lectures, presentations, expert testimony, and support for attending meetings and/or travel from Phenox/Wallaby and eppdata GmbH; and stock ownership in NVIDIA. DMH reports grants from the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft, unrelated to the submitted work; honoraria for lectures and presentations from Eisai GmbH; support for meeting attendance and/or travel from DGNR and ESNR; and a leadership role on the ESNR AI Committee. DS reports travel and accommodation support from BALT Germany to attend a workshop. JB reports support for attending meetings and/or travel from BALT and Terumo Neuro. JSK reports grants from the German Research Foundation, the European Research Council, Bayern Innovativ, and the Bayerische Forschungsstiftung (all paid to institution); honoraria for lectures and presentations from Novartis in 2022; and stock ownership in Bonescreen GmbH (co-founder). JR reports honoraria for an industry-related lecture from Terumo Neuro at DGNR 2024; and support for attending industry-related workshops in Essen and Zurich in 2025 from Terumo Neuro. MTB-M reports grants or contracts from Terumo Neuro Germany, BALT International, and Stryker GmbH & Co. KG; consulting fees from Terumo Neuro Germany, BALT International, and Stryker GmbH & Co. KG; honoraria for lectures and presentations from Terumo Neuro Germany, BALT International, and Stryker GmbH & Co. KG; and support for attending meetings and/or travel from Terumo Neuro Germany, BALT International, and Stryker GmbH & Co. KG. TB-B reports honoraria for lectures and presentations from Terumo Neuro and BALT; and support for meeting attendance and/or travel from BALT and Phenox/Wallaby.
